title: Bendin In The Wind

jokes.text: Oh, my God! They dug up a bag of olestra chips from my time!
jokes.setup: Archaeological dig uncovers artifacts from the past
jokes.punchline: The 'artifact' is a bag of olestra chips, a mundane snack food from the 1990s
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor - only Fry would be excited about finding his era's junk food. The absurdity of olestra chips being treated as archaeological treasure works well.

jokes.text: Is that one of those Led Zeppelins? - No, it's an old Volkswagen van!
jokes.setup: Character sees an old van and tries to identify it
jokes.punchline: Confuses 'Led Zeppelin' (the band) with an actual zeppelin aircraft when looking at a VW van
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay confusion. Works as intended but not particularly sophisticated or character-specific.

jokes.text: Mind if I take this old van? Sure. You wanna dump the corpses out of there, it's yours. Yeah, I've gotten used cars before.
jokes.setup: Fry asks to take an old van
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of corpses, followed by Bender's nonchalant acceptance as if this is normal used car experience
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect Bender character integration. The casual delivery of the corpses line followed by Bender's matter-of-fact response creates great comedic contrast.

jokes.text: What's that? One of those Jefferson Starships? It's called a van. And in light of the fact it's not rocking, I invite you to come knocking.
jokes.setup: Another misidentified vehicle reference to a band name
jokes.punchline: Fry's awkward attempt at the 'if the van's a-rockin'' joke falls flat because the van isn't rocking
jokes.explanation: Good setup-punchline structure with Fry's characteristic awkwardness. The Jefferson Starship/van confusion continues the pattern from joke #2.

jokes.text: It's a triumph of German engineering! The speedometer only goes up to 80. It can't go faster than 80,000 mph?
jokes.setup: Fry praises German engineering of the VW van
jokes.punchline: Assumes the 80 mph limit means 80,000 mph, showing his future-world perspective where everything goes much faster
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific humor showing Fry's fish-out-of-water status. The assumption that 80 means 80,000 perfectly captures his adjustment to future technology.

jokes.text: No, but it's got a driver's-side floor and an eight-track player!
jokes.setup: Response to speed question about the van's features
jokes.punchline: Having a floor on the driver's side is presented as a selling point, implying it's unusual
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor - the idea that having a floor is a special feature suggests how decrepit the van is.

jokes.text: Where's the device that lets you speed or slow the passage of time? Under the seat.
jokes.setup: Future character asks about a time manipulation device in the van
jokes.punchline: It's under the seat (likely referring to drugs/alcohol, a mundane explanation for a sci-fi question)
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and misdirection. The setup sounds like sci-fi technobabble but the punchline implies drugs/alcohol, a very hippie van reference.

jokes.text: Why won't it start? - It needs gas. Wrong again, idiot. There is no gas. Petroleum reserves ran dry in 2038.
jokes.setup: Van won't start, Fry suggests it needs gas
jokes.punchline: Bender calls him an idiot and explains petroleum ran out in 2038, showing Fry's outdated knowledge
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction showing Fry's outdated knowledge and Bender's blunt corrections. Establishes world-building while getting a laugh.

jokes.text: Gas was an environmental disaster. We use alternative fuels. - Like what? - Whale oil.
jokes.setup: Character explains they use alternative fuels instead of environmentally disastrous gas
jokes.punchline: The 'alternative' fuel is whale oil, which is even more environmentally problematic
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic reversal. The future has solved environmental problems by going back to an even worse environmental solution. Perfect setup-punchline structure.

jokes.text: Last time, that magnetic psycho nearly cut my head off! Plus the magnet impairs your inhibition unit and makes you sing folk songs.
jokes.setup: Bender complains about the dangerous can opener
jokes.punchline: The real concern is that it makes him sing folk songs due to impairing his inhibition unit
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific humor that sets up the episode's main plot. The idea that Bender's inhibitions prevent folk singing is perfect robot psychology comedy.

jokes.text: What? Who said anything about me secretly wanting to be a folk singer?
jokes.setup: No one mentioned Bender wanting to be a folk singer
jokes.punchline: Bender defensively reveals this secret desire by denying something nobody said
jokes.explanation: Classic 'lady doth protest too much' structure. Bender accidentally reveals his secret by overreacting to something nobody mentioned.

jokes.text: It's time to take life by the cans.
jokes.setup: Bender psyching himself up to face the can opener
jokes.punchline: 'Take life by the horns' becomes 'take life by the cans' since he's dealing with cans
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective pun. The wordplay fits the situation and Bender's character, though it's not particularly sophisticated.

jokes.text: It's toe-tappingly tragic!
jokes.setup: Bender gets mangled by the can opener and starts making rhythmic sounds
jokes.punchline: The alliterative description captures both the musical quality and the tragic nature
jokes.explanation: Good alliterative phrase that captures the duality of the situation - tragic but musical. Economical and memorable.

jokes.text: This is the worst part of the job. - Good news? Bender, your hydraulics are shot. You'll never move again.
jokes.setup: Doctor says this is the worst part of his job, Fry hopefully asks 'Good news?'
jokes.punchline: The 'good news' delivery style is used to deliver devastating news about permanent paralysis
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal using the 'good news/bad news' format. The setup creates expectation for actual good news, making the devastating diagnosis more impactful.

jokes.text: You mean...? - Sorry, you'll have to get a new one.
jokes.setup: Bender processing the diagnosis about never moving again
jokes.punchline: Doctor casually suggests getting a 'new one' as if Bender is a replaceable appliance
jokes.explanation: Dark humor treating Bender as a replaceable object rather than a person. Good economy and fits the show's robot-human relationship dynamics.

jokes.text: Poor Bender! - Well, let's drag him to the curb.
jokes.setup: Fry expresses sympathy for Bender
jokes.punchline: Immediately followed by Leela's practical suggestion to dispose of him
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast between Fry's emotion and Leela's pragmatism

jokes.text: We can't just dump him in the gutter like Grandma's ashes.
jokes.setup: Fry defending Bender from being thrown away
jokes.punchline: The comparison is to dumping Grandma's ashes in the gutter, implying this happened
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with great character integration. Shows Fry's loyalty while casually revealing disturbing family history. The comparison makes his defense of Bender both touching and horrifying.

jokes.text: Why do they use Windows 3000 as a prison guard? Because it always locks up.
jokes.setup: Setup for a computer joke about Windows 3000
jokes.punchline: 'Locks up' works as both computer freezing and prison security
jokes.explanation: Generic computer joke with predictable punchline. The Windows reference updates it for the future but it's still a standard dad-joke structure.

jokes.text: For God's sake, somebody kick his ass!
jokes.setup: Patch Cord Adams delivers a terrible joke
jokes.punchline: Bender's violent reaction to bad comedy
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Bender's intolerance for bad entertainment even when he's paralyzed. The violent reaction to terrible comedy is very Bender.

jokes.text: Hey, the blues! The tragic sound of other people suffering. That's kind of a pick-me-up.
jokes.setup: Bender hears blues music being played
jokes.punchline: He finds other people's suffering uplifting, which is darkly in character
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender character moment. His sociopathic finding joy in others' misery is both horrible and hilarious. Sets up his interest in music through his twisted psychology.

jokes.text: I'd get up to shake hands and steal your wallet, but my body crapped out.
jokes.setup: Bender meeting Beck and explaining why he can't get up
jokes.punchline: Casually admits he would steal Beck's wallet as part of a normal greeting
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing Bender's criminal nature as completely casual and normal to him. The politeness of the greeting contrasted with admitted theft is perfect Bender.

jokes.text: I used to miss my body too... but then I fished this mannequin out of the 92¢ Store dumpster.
jokes.setup: Beck relating to Bender's body issues
jokes.punchline: Reveals his current body is a mannequin from a discount store dumpster
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist humor with good visual comedy potential. The specificity of '92¢ Store' and the casual delivery make it funnier. Shows how the future Beck has adapted.

jokes.text: Try and scrape it across your chest like a knife on burnt toast.
jokes.setup: Beck teaching Bender how to play washboard
jokes.punchline: The specific and unappetizing comparison to scraping burnt toast
jokes.explanation: Good descriptive humor with a specific, relatable but unpleasant comparison. The burnt toast imagery is vivid and adds texture to the instruction.

jokes.text: Why, 'cause I made an annoying noise? - I use those all the time in my music.
jokes.setup: Bender questions why Beck is congratulating him for making noise
jokes.punchline: Beck admits he regularly uses annoying noises in his music
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about experimental music. Beck's character acknowledging that his music contains 'annoying noises' is both self-deprecating and accurate to his real musical style.

jokes.text: Kudos, Bender! You got mangled, and now you're a singer. Both our dreams came true.
jokes.setup: Fry celebrating Bender's musical success
jokes.punchline: Frames getting mangled as the path to both their dreams coming true
jokes.explanation: Good ironic observation about how disaster led to success. The cheerful delivery of dark content is characteristically optimistic Fry.

jokes.text: I don't know, Fry. I think I'm too poor to follow a band around in a van.
jokes.setup: Fry suggesting they follow Bender on tour
jokes.punchline: Leela thinks she's too poor for what's typically an extremely poor lifestyle choice
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic humor - being too poor for a lifestyle associated with being broke. Good character moment showing Leela's practical nature.

jokes.text: Bad news, friends. My shell ran. - Zoidberg, you idiot!
jokes.setup: Zoidberg announces his shell ran (stockings got a run)
jokes.punchline: Visual gag of crustacean in drag plus angry reaction
jokes.explanation: Great visual gag mixing Zoidberg's shell with women's hosiery, perfect character absurdity

jokes.text: My outfit! It's... - Kind of cool! - I like it! - Me too, now that I'm used to it. Then it was all on purpose! You're lucky to have me as a friend.
jokes.setup: Zoidberg's shell/outfit gets ruined, others unexpectedly like it
jokes.punchline: Zoidberg immediately takes credit as if it was intentional, then threatens to turn on them
jokes.explanation: Perfect Zoidberg character moment - taking credit for an accident and immediately switching to mild threat. The quick pivot from grateful to threatening is classic Zoidberg.

jokes.text: But cross me, and I'll turn on you like that!
jokes.setup: Zoidberg being friendly and grateful
jokes.punchline: Sudden threat of betrayal
jokes.explanation: Good Zoidberg character beat - switching from grateful to threatening unpredictably

jokes.text: One bowl of Jell-O knuckles. That'll be three pictures of George Washington.
jokes.setup: Ordering food at what appears to be a hippie establishment
jokes.punchline: 'Pictures of George Washington' instead of dollar bills, plus the gross food item 'Jell-O knuckles'
jokes.explanation: Good world-building humor with hippie vernacular and disgusting food. The George Washington reference is a nice touch for the hippie setting.

jokes.text: Oh, no! My beautiful money! - It got ruined in the wash! - Mine too, even my change.
jokes.setup: Money getting ruined in washing
jokes.punchline: Bender's change also got ruined, implying he washes his coins
jokes.explanation: Minor character humor about Bender washing his change. The escalation from paper money to coins adds a small layer but it's not particularly strong.

jokes.text: Get lost, you moneyless hippies! You heard him, freaks! We don't like your type around here! These are the types we like.
jokes.setup: Vendor rejecting the crew as hippies
jokes.punchline: The 'acceptable types' are presumably actual dirty hippies
jokes.explanation: Ironic reversal - the clean future people are rejected in favor of actual hippies

jokes.text: That was the best 40-minute washboard solo I've ever heard. The parts when I was awake blew my mind!
jokes.setup: Fan praising Bender's performance
jokes.punchline: Admits they slept through most of the 40-minute solo but still calls it the best
jokes.explanation: Good satire of pretentious music criticism and overly long performances. The honesty about sleeping through it while still praising it captures hipster culture well.

jokes.text: I don't believe my broken eyes! It's Bender, the washboard player!
jokes.setup: Robot fan recognizing Bender
jokes.punchline: Says 'broken eyes' instead of 'eyes' because he's a broken robot
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that makes sense for the character. Functional but not outstanding.

jokes.text: That's so wrong! They can't just melt down broken robots! - Not right when they're kissing my ass.
jokes.setup: Bender outraged at injustice to robots
jokes.punchline: His real concern is losing his fan base
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender - what seems like moral outrage is actually selfish concern about losing admirers

jokes.text: Yeah, I could write a song! With real words, not phony ones like 'odelay.' - Odelay is a word. Look it up in the Becktionary.
jokes.setup: Bender wants to write a song with real words, dismisses 'odelay' as phony
jokes.punchline: Beck defends it by referencing the 'Becktionary', his own made-up dictionary
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor about Beck's actual music. 'Becktionary' is a brilliant portmanteau that became a cultural reference. Perfect character integration with Beck defending his artistic choices.

jokes.text: I'm working on my song. Hand me the Becktionary. No, the rhyming Becktionary.
jokes.setup: Bender asking for the Becktionary
jokes.punchline: There's also a rhyming version, suggesting an entire series of Beck dictionaries
jokes.explanation: Good callback that expands the joke. The idea of multiple specialized Becktionaries escalates the absurdity effectively.

jokes.text: I'd like to raise awareness about broken robots... but what can I do? I only weigh 8 pounds.
jokes.setup: Bender wants to help broken robots but feels helpless
jokes.punchline: His measurement of helplessness is his low weight rather than his paralysis
jokes.explanation: Good character humor showing Bender's odd priorities. Focusing on weight rather than paralysis as the limiting factor is perfectly robotic logic.

jokes.text: Can we crash here? The colors in the van keep us awake.
jokes.setup: Asking to stay over
jokes.punchline: Complaint about colors keeping them awake (presumably tie-dye patterns)
jokes.explanation: Absurd complaint about psychedelic van decorations, fits the hippie setting

jokes.text: Can we crash here? The colors in the van keep us awake.
jokes.setup: Asking to stay at someone's place
jokes.punchline: Colors are keeping them awake (implying psychedelic experience)
jokes.explanation: Good hippie culture joke. The absurd reason for not sleeping fits the drug-influenced lifestyle being parodied.

jokes.text: A portion of the proceeds might go to help broken robots.
jokes.setup: Advertisement for the benefit concert
jokes.punchline: Only a vague 'portion' 'might' go to charity, showing typical corporate charity cynicism
jokes.explanation: Good satire of charity event marketing. The non-committal language ('portion', 'might') effectively parodies corporate charity skepticism.

jokes.text: A portion of the proceeds might go to help broken robots.
jokes.setup: Charity concert announcement
jokes.punchline: Only a 'portion' 'might' go to charity - very noncommittal
jokes.explanation: Good satire of vague charity promises. The double uncertainty (portion + might) effectively mocks corporate charity.

jokes.text: Bender! You can move! You're cured! - Oh, crap! It's a miracle.
jokes.setup: Someone celebrates Bender being cured
jokes.punchline: Bender responds to his miracle cure with 'Oh, crap!' showing it's actually bad news for him
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic reversal with perfect character integration. Bender's dismay at being cured subverts expectations perfectly. The deadpan 'Oh, crap! It's a miracle' is brilliantly crafted.

jokes.text: That's like Christina Aguilera singing in Spanish.
jokes.setup: Bender explaining why he can't sing about being damaged if he's not
jokes.punchline: Compares it to Christina Aguilera singing in Spanish (which she actually does)
jokes.explanation: Decent pop culture reference but the comparison doesn't quite land since Christina Aguilera did sing in Spanish. The intent is clear but the example undermines itself.

jokes.text: They're beautiful! - You're touching them! I've never seen pearls like this. You're amazing! - I am? At last! Recognition!
jokes.setup: Amy and Leela discover Zoidberg has been making beautiful pearls
jokes.punchline: Zoidberg's desperate gratitude for any recognition of his abilities
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Zoidberg's desperate need for validation. His pathetic excitement at any recognition is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Look at the colors! These will go great with my soul. Glad you like them. I've been making fine jewelry for years, apparently.
jokes.setup: Hippie admiring Zoidberg's pearls
jokes.punchline: Zoidberg's casual 'apparently' about his new jewelry career
jokes.explanation: Good Zoidberg deadpan about suddenly being an expert jeweler 'apparently'

jokes.text: That song doesn't usually last three hours... but we got into a serious thing, and then I forgot how it ended.
jokes.setup: Beck explaining why their performance went so long
jokes.punchline: Casual admission that they got carried away and forgot the ending, extending a song to three hours
jokes.explanation: Great satire of jam bands and experimental music. Beck's casual delivery of this ridiculous explanation perfectly captures the pretentious music scene.

jokes.text: here's a big cardboard check for $14,000.
jokes.setup: Beck presenting money to Bender for his charity
jokes.punchline: It's a comically large cardboard check for a relatively small amount of money
jokes.explanation: Standard visual gag about oversized checks. The humor comes from the contrast between the dramatic presentation and modest amount.

jokes.text: You big fat fraud! - You're not really broken! - But you will be!
jokes.setup: Crowd discovers Bender has been faking his disability
jokes.punchline: Threat that he will be broken for real as punishment
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the mob justice theme. The threat creates poetic justice - he'll become what he was pretending to be.

jokes.text: What is this, the Year of the Jerk?!
jokes.setup: Someone reacting to all the chaos and bad behavior
jokes.punchline: Suggests there's an official 'Year of the Jerk' like Chinese zodiac years
jokes.explanation: Good reactive comedy with nice economy. The idea of an official 'Year of the Jerk' is absurd and memorable.

jokes.text: What is this, the Year of the Jerk?!
jokes.setup: Bender being chased by angry crowd
jokes.punchline: Bender complaining about jerks while being the biggest jerk
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender irony - the actual jerk complaining about jerks, great self-awareness failure

jokes.text: I forgot it's a hoverbridge! - I forgot this isn't a hover car! - Is that a problem?
jokes.setup: Fry realizes the bridge hovers, Leela realizes their car doesn't
jokes.punchline: Fry's oblivious question about whether this is a problem
jokes.explanation: Good escalating disaster with perfect Fry obliviousness. His clueless question in the face of obvious danger is classic Fry character comedy.

jokes.text: Someone fat, get in my way!
jokes.setup: Bender falling and looking for something to break his fall
jokes.punchline: His specific request for someone fat to cushion his landing
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bender's selfishness and practicality. His specific requirement for a fat person is both rude and logical for cushioning.

jokes.text: That's some played-out horsecrap! But I know you don't mean it. Apology accepted.
jokes.setup: Beck calling out Bender's terrible apology
jokes.punchline: Immediately accepts it anyway, showing his laid-back nature
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Beck's forgiving nature. The quick reversal from criticism to acceptance fits his mellow personality.

jokes.text: Well, it was worth a shot.
jokes.setup: Beck refuses to let Bender keep the charity money
jokes.punchline: Bender's casual acceptance that his attempt to keep stolen charity money failed
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender moment showing his shameless opportunism. His casual attitude about trying to steal charity money is perfectly in character.

tmdb_overview: When Bender is paralyzed in a tragic can opener accident, he discovers his musical washboard skills and goes on tour as a member of Beck's folk-rock band, acting as a voice for broken robots everywhere. Fry, Leela, Amy, and Zoidberg tag along in Fry's antique 1960s VW Van.
